verses.text: Two more years pass. Then Pharaoh has a dream: seven sleek, well-fed cows come up out of the Nile, followed by seven thin, ugly cows. The thin ones eat the fat ones — and remain just as thin. He wakes up.

Two more years pass. Then Pharaoh has a dream: seven sleek, well-fed cows come up out of the Nile, followed by seven thin, ugly cows. The thin ones eat the fat ones — and remain just as thin. He wakes up.

Two more years pass. Then Pharaoh has a dream: seven sleek, well-fed cows come up out of the Nile, followed by seven thin, ugly cows. The thin ones eat the fat ones — and remain just as thin. He wakes up.

Two more years pass. Then Pharaoh has a dream: seven sleek, well-fed cows come up out of the Nile, followed by seven thin, ugly cows. The thin ones eat the fat ones — and remain just as thin. He wakes up.

He falls asleep again, and dreams again. Seven plump, healthy ears of grain on a single stalk; then seven thin ears, scorched by the east wind, swallow them up. Pharaoh wakes, and behold — it was a dream.

He falls asleep again, and dreams again. Seven plump, healthy ears of grain on a single stalk; then seven thin ears, scorched by the east wind, swallow them up. Pharaoh wakes, and behold — it was a dream.

He falls asleep again, and dreams again. Seven plump, healthy ears of grain on a single stalk; then seven thin ears, scorched by the east wind, swallow them up. Pharaoh wakes, and behold — it was a dream.
In the morning, his spirit is troubled. He calls every magician and wise man of Egypt and tells them the dreams. None of them can interpret it. The wisest court in the ancient world is stumped.
Then the chief cupbearer finally speaks: I remember my faults today. He recalls the year Pharaoh's anger put him and the baker in prison.
Then the chief cupbearer finally speaks: I remember my faults today. He recalls the year Pharaoh's anger put him and the baker in prison.
There was a young Hebrew man in the prison, servant of the captain of the guard. He interpreted our dreams, and it happened exactly as he said. The forgotten name resurfaces at the perfect moment.
There was a young Hebrew man in the prison, servant of the captain of the guard. He interpreted our dreams, and it happened exactly as he said. The forgotten name resurfaces at the perfect moment.
There was a young Hebrew man in the prison, servant of the captain of the guard. He interpreted our dreams, and it happened exactly as he said. The forgotten name resurfaces at the perfect moment.

Pharaoh sends, and they bring Joseph in haste out of the dungeon. He shaves — Egyptian style — and changes his clothes, and goes in before Pharaoh. Yesterday he was a prisoner; today he is in the throne room.

I have dreamed a dream, says Pharaoh, and no one can interpret it. I have heard you can. Joseph answers: it is not in me. God shall give Pharaoh an answer of peace. The very first words he speaks to the king of Egypt point past himself to God.

I have dreamed a dream, says Pharaoh, and no one can interpret it. I have heard you can. Joseph answers: it is not in me. God shall give Pharaoh an answer of peace. The very first words he speaks to the king of Egypt point past himself to God.
Pharaoh tells the dream of the cows: seven fat ones grazing by the Nile, then seven lean ones, the ugliest and poorest he has ever seen in Egypt.
Pharaoh tells the dream of the cows: seven fat ones grazing by the Nile, then seven lean ones, the ugliest and poorest he has ever seen in Egypt.
Pharaoh tells the dream of the cows: seven fat ones grazing by the Nile, then seven lean ones, the ugliest and poorest he has ever seen in Egypt.
The lean cows eat the fat ones — and remain just as lean as before. Even after the meal, they look like they haven't eaten. So I awoke.
The lean cows eat the fat ones — and remain just as lean as before. Even after the meal, they look like they haven't eaten. So I awoke.

Then the second dream: seven good ears of grain on one stalk, swallowed up by seven thin, withered ears scorched by the east wind. He told it to the magicians; not one could explain it. Pharaoh has been carrying these dreams alone.

Then the second dream: seven good ears of grain on one stalk, swallowed up by seven thin, withered ears scorched by the east wind. He told it to the magicians; not one could explain it. Pharaoh has been carrying these dreams alone.

Then the second dream: seven good ears of grain on one stalk, swallowed up by seven thin, withered ears scorched by the east wind. He told it to the magicians; not one could explain it. Pharaoh has been carrying these dreams alone.
Joseph answers: the dream of Pharaoh is one dream. God has shown Pharaoh what He is about to do. He treats both dreams as one message — a doubled emphasis from God.
The seven good cows and the seven good ears are seven years. The seven lean cows and the seven empty ears are seven years also — years of famine. Two pictures, one message: plenty followed by lack.
The seven good cows and the seven good ears are seven years. The seven lean cows and the seven empty ears are seven years also — years of famine. Two pictures, one message: plenty followed by lack.
What God is about to do, He has shown to Pharaoh. Seven years of great plenty are coming. Then seven years of famine so severe that all the plenty will be forgotten in the land.
What God is about to do, He has shown to Pharaoh. Seven years of great plenty are coming. Then seven years of famine so severe that all the plenty will be forgotten in the land.
What God is about to do, He has shown to Pharaoh. Seven years of great plenty are coming. Then seven years of famine so severe that all the plenty will be forgotten in the land.
The famine will consume the land so thoroughly that no one will remember the abundance. The dream was doubled because the matter is established by God — He will bring it to pass shortly.
The famine will consume the land so thoroughly that no one will remember the abundance. The dream was doubled because the matter is established by God — He will bring it to pass shortly.

Now let Pharaoh find a discerning and wise man, and set him over the land. Appoint overseers; take a fifth of every harvest in the years of plenty; store grain under Pharaoh's authority in every city. That food will keep the land alive when the famine comes.

Now let Pharaoh find a discerning and wise man, and set him over the land. Appoint overseers; take a fifth of every harvest in the years of plenty; store grain under Pharaoh's authority in every city. That food will keep the land alive when the famine comes.

Now let Pharaoh find a discerning and wise man, and set him over the land. Appoint overseers; take a fifth of every harvest in the years of plenty; store grain under Pharaoh's authority in every city. That food will keep the land alive when the famine comes.

Now let Pharaoh find a discerning and wise man, and set him over the land. Appoint overseers; take a fifth of every harvest in the years of plenty; store grain under Pharaoh's authority in every city. That food will keep the land alive when the famine comes.

The plan is good in Pharaoh's eyes and in the eyes of all his servants. Can we find anyone like this, asks Pharaoh — a man in whom the Spirit of God is? To Joseph: since God has shown you all this, none so discerning as you. You shall be over my house. Only in the throne will I be greater than you.

The plan is good in Pharaoh's eyes and in the eyes of all his servants. Can we find anyone like this, asks Pharaoh — a man in whom the Spirit of God is? To Joseph: since God has shown you all this, none so discerning as you. You shall be over my house. Only in the throne will I be greater than you.

Pharaoh sets Joseph over all of Egypt. He takes his signet ring off his own hand and puts it on Joseph's. He clothes him in fine linen, puts a gold chain around his neck, and rides him in the chariot just behind his own. The cry goes before him: Bow the knee.

Pharaoh sets Joseph over all of Egypt. He takes his signet ring off his own hand and puts it on Joseph's. He clothes him in fine linen, puts a gold chain around his neck, and rides him in the chariot just behind his own. The cry goes before him: Bow the knee.

Pharaoh sets Joseph over all of Egypt. He takes his signet ring off his own hand and puts it on Joseph's. He clothes him in fine linen, puts a gold chain around his neck, and rides him in the chariot just behind his own. The cry goes before him: Bow the knee.

I am Pharaoh, he says — and without you no one shall lift hand or foot in all Egypt. He gives Joseph an Egyptian name, Zaphnath-paaneah, and an Egyptian wife, Asenath daughter of the priest of On. Joseph's new life is fully arranged in a single day.

I am Pharaoh, he says — and without you no one shall lift hand or foot in all Egypt. He gives Joseph an Egyptian name, Zaphnath-paaneah, and an Egyptian wife, Asenath daughter of the priest of On. Joseph's new life is fully arranged in a single day.
Joseph is thirty years old when he stands before Pharaoh. Thirteen years have passed since the pit. He goes out across all the land of Egypt — the same age David would later become king.

In the seven good years the earth brings forth by handfuls. Joseph gathers all the food and stores it in every city. Eventually he stops counting the grain, for it is like the sand of the sea — beyond measure.

In the seven good years the earth brings forth by handfuls. Joseph gathers all the food and stores it in every city. Eventually he stops counting the grain, for it is like the sand of the sea — beyond measure.

In the seven good years the earth brings forth by handfuls. Joseph gathers all the food and stores it in every city. Eventually he stops counting the grain, for it is like the sand of the sea — beyond measure.

Before the famine begins, Asenath bears Joseph two sons. He names the first Manasseh — for God has made me forget all my toil and my father's house. The second he names Ephraim — for God has caused me to be fruitful in the land of my affliction. Two names that finally close two wounds.

Before the famine begins, Asenath bears Joseph two sons. He names the first Manasseh — for God has made me forget all my toil and my father's house. The second he names Ephraim — for God has caused me to be fruitful in the land of my affliction. Two names that finally close two wounds.

Before the famine begins, Asenath bears Joseph two sons. He names the first Manasseh — for God has made me forget all my toil and my father's house. The second he names Ephraim — for God has caused me to be fruitful in the land of my affliction. Two names that finally close two wounds.
The seven plenteous years come to an end, and the seven years of famine begin — exactly as Joseph said. The famine spreads to every land. But in all the land of Egypt there is bread.
The seven plenteous years come to an end, and the seven years of famine begin — exactly as Joseph said. The famine spreads to every land. But in all the land of Egypt there is bread.

When the people cry to Pharaoh for food, he says to all Egypt: go to Joseph; whatever he tells you, do. Joseph opens the storehouses and sells grain to the Egyptians. And all countries come to Egypt to buy from Joseph, because the famine is severe everywhere on earth.
